summary: Apple has released a software workaround for the blood oxygen sensor ban affecting Apple Watch models sold in the US after January 2024. The workaround, enabled by a new US Customs ruling, processes and displays blood oxygen data on the paired iPhone rather than on the watch itself, which apparently sidesteps Masimo's patents. Gruber explains the technical details of how the workaround functions and notes that users outside the US or with pre-ban watches are unaffected. He also highlights a confusing aspect: an additional over-the-air asset download is needed after the software update, which Apple's announcement didn't make clear. Gruber concludes by reiterating his view that Masimo is a patent troll attempting to monetize patents expiring in 2028 by extorting a settlement from Apple.
key_points: The workaround moves blood oxygen data processing and display from the Apple Watch to the paired iPhone, which US Customs ruled does not violate Masimo's patents
Users with pre-ban US watches or watches purchased outside the US retain the original on-device blood oxygen experience and are unaffected
After the software update, an additional over-the-air asset download (potentially taking up to 24 hours) is required to enable the feature — a detail Apple's announcement omitted
The US Customs ruling implies Masimo's patents only cover on-device sensor processing and display of results, not the sensor measurements themselves
Gruber views Masimo as a patent troll that filed its ITC complaint before even having a smartwatch product, with patents expiring in 2028
money_quotes: What today's workaround does is process and display the blood oxygen sensor data on your watch's paired iPhone, rather than on the Apple Watch itself.
I continue to think that Masimo is a patent troll.
At the time they filed their complaint with the ITC, they didn't even have a smartwatch on the market.
The smartwatch they now sell — years after filing the complaint — looks like an Apple Watch with a second button instead of a digital crown.
The two patents in question are set to expire in August 2028, and I suspect this patent suit has been a last-ditch attempt to monetize them before they expire by extorting a settlement from Apple.
Reading between the lines, the implication is that US Customs has decided that Masimo's patents only apply to on-device sensor processing.
key_insight: Apple's blood oxygen workaround cleverly sidesteps Masimo's patents by shifting data processing from the watch to the iPhone, underscoring Gruber's view that Masimo's patent suit is a last-ditch monetization attempt before the patents expire in 2028.
